metadata.title: The Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack Housing Development
metadata.author: Alexa Richman
metadata.subtitle: A Study Over Time

caption.text: Old Harbor Village, also called the Mary Ellen McCormack Housing Development, was New England’s first public housing development, built in 1936 and opened in 1938. Located in Southie between the Andrew and JFK stops on the MBTA Red Line, the development was built on a formerly unused site of 31 acres.

As of summer 2025, ground broke on a $2 billion renovation plan made possible by the Boston Housing Authority, the developer WinnDevelopment, and an elected tenant association, the Mary Ellen McCormack Task Force. Officials have promised that all current Mary Ellen McCormack residents who are required to relocate temporarily for the Redevelopment Project have the right to return and to be rehoused in an appropriately sized new unit at the Mary Ellen McCormack site.

This is not the housing development’s first renovation project since its opening in 1938. What was the neighborhood like before the construction of Old Harbor Village? What steps have these new renovation projects taken to incorporate features of the old neighborhood into the new one?

This is an 1882 map of the land that Old Harbor Village would eventually be built on. From the 1820s onward, South Boston became home to a large Irish population, although Canadians, Germans, Poles, Lithuanians, and Russian Jews also immigrated to the area. Although many may have been grateful for the opportunity to move into subsidized housing in the early twentieth century, some may have had mixed feelings about the change to their culturally diverse neighborhood.

The housing development sits directly beside Carson Beach. As early as the late 1800s, the Boston sewer system began to divert raw sewage into the Boston Harbor near Moon Island. Most of the Boston coastline became polluted, including Carson Beach. The city began to clean its waters in 1985, yet for much of Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack Housing Development’s history, proximity to the beach was not always desirable.

This is a house on Kemp Street, a few blocks north of Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack, in 1898. This type of house design was common in South Boston and continued to reflect the ways in which houses on the outskirts of the development may have appeared and evolved throughout the twentieth century.

A map of the neighborhood directly north of Old Harbor Village, where the house on Kemp Street was built. Notice its proximity to Telegraph Hill, the tallest hill in South Boston, on which there is a monument tower dedicated to Dorchester Heights and the American Revolution. This neighborhood was evidently one around which many cultures and national celebrations were centered.
This is a 1910 map of the land that Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack would be built on in two decades’ time.

Living New Deal reports that the housing development was built on 31 acres of unused land. However, in reality, not all of that land was unused. Some of it pressed right up against existing neighborhoods, and much of that land was privately owned. The Massachusetts Horticultural Society building was displaced, as were properties owned by John J. Collins, William Cotton, George B. Cushing, Frederick F. Ayer, and others.

Around the time of the public housing project’s construction, the Home Owners Loan Corporation (HOLC), in partnership with FDR, created redlining maps, or color-coded maps of American cities which revealed the “safest” or “riskiest” urban areas. Neighborhoods that were deemed safe investments were given a grade of A and colored green; those that were deemed “hazardous” were given a grade of D and colored red. These maps ultimately reinforced racist stereotypes, as African American, immigrant, and Jewish residents were deemed threats to the stability of home values, and their presence was considered an “infiltration.”
